20:T41a,function findEnclosingLoop(node: SgNode<JS>): SgNode<JS> | null {
  let current = node.parent();
  while (current) {
    const kind = current.kind();
    if (
      kind === "for_statement" ||
      kind === "for_in_statement" ||
      kind === "while_statement"
    ) {
      return current;
    }
    // Stop at function boundaries
    if (
      kind === "function_declaration" ||
      kind === "function_expression" ||
      kind === "arrow_function" ||
      kind === "program"
    ) {
      return null;
    }
    current = current.parent();
  }
  return null;
}

function isInLoopWithClosure(node: SgNode<JS>): boolean {
  const loop = findEnclosingLoop(node);
  if (!loop) return false;

  // If the loop contains any closures, keep all var declarations
  // as var to preserve the original binding behavior.
  const closures = loop.findAll({
    rule: {
      any: [
        { kind: "function_declaration" },
        { kind: "function_expression" },
        { kind: "arrow_function" },
      ],
    },

const transform: Transform<JS> = (root) => {
  const rootNode = root.root();
  const edits = [];

  // `var $DECL` structurally matches any variable_declaration whose
  // first token is the `var` keyword — no text filtering needed.

    if (varKeyword) {
      edits.push(varKeyword.replace(newKind));
    }
  }

  // Tree-sitter does not wrap `for (var x of items)` in a variable_declaration.

  // We match structurally with patterns and replace only that keyword token.
  const forOfVars = rootNode.findAll({
    rule: { pattern: "for (var $X of $Y) $BODY" },
  });
  for (const forOf of forOfVars) {
    const varKeyword = forOf.child(2);
    if (varKeyword?.kind() === "var") {
      edits.push(varKeyword.replace("const"));
    }
  }

  const forInVars = rootNode.findAll({
    rule: { pattern: "for (var $X in $Y) $BODY" },
  });
  for (const forIn of forInVars) {
    const varKeyword = forIn.child(2);
    if (varKeyword?.kind() === "var") {
      edits.push(varKeyword.replace("const"));
    }
  }

  if (edits.length === 0) return null;

  return rootNode.commitEdits(edits);
};

py-5">Prerequisites</h2></div><ul class="my-2 ml-4 list-disc [&>li]:mt-1"><li><strong>Understanding ASTs</strong> — This article requires a basic understanding of how to inspect, traverse, and manipulate Abstract Syntax Trees. <a target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" class="underline underline-offset-[3px] inline-flex items-center gap-0.5 group pr-0.5 text-foreground" href="https://app.codemod.com/studio">Codemod Studio<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" class="lucide lucide-arrow-up-right size-4 group-hover:translate-x-[2px] group-hover:-translate-y-[2px] transition-transform ease-out" aria-hidden="true"><path d="M7 7h10v10"></path><path d="M7 17 17 7"></path></svg></a> has a built-in AST tree explorer that's great for visualizing node structures interactively.</li><li><strong>Codemod CLI installed</strong> — You'll need the Codemod CLI available via <code>npx codemod</code>. See the <a target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" class="underline underline-offset-[3px] inline-flex items-center gap-0.5 group pr-0.5 text-foreground" href="https://docs.codemod.com/cli">CLI docs<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" class="lucide 7"></path></svg></a> if you haven't set it up yet.</li></ul><div class="relative group flex items-center"><a href="#overview" aria-label="Link to overview" class="absolute -left-6 opacity-0 group-hover:opacity-100 transition-opacity text-muted-foreground hover:text-foreground no-underline select-none"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" scroll-mt-24 text-xl sm:text-2xl lg:text-3xl py-5">Overview</h2></div><p class="mb-4">Throughout this guide, we'll break down the thought process behind writing a real-world codemod—from identifying patterns and planning edge cases to implementing and testing the transform.</p><p class="mb-4">By the end, you will learn:</p><ul class="my-2 ml-4 list-disc [&>li]:mt-1"><li>How to write a codemod that solves a real-world problem using <a target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" class="underline underline-offset-[3px] inline-flex items-center gap-0.5 group pr-0.5 text-foreground" href="https://docs.codemod.com/jssg/quickstart">JSSG<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" class="lucide lucide-arrow-up-right size-4 group-hover:translate-x-[2px] group-hover:-translate-y-[2px] transition-transform ease-out" aria-hidden="true"><path d="M7 7h10v10"></path><path d="M7 17 17 7"></path></svg></a>, Codemod's transformation engine.</li><li>How to use ast-grep pattern matching and AST manipulation techniques.</li><li>How to test and publish your codemod using the Codemod CLI.</li></ul><p class="mb-4">Let's learn by example!</p><div class="relative group flex items-center"><a href="#problem" aria-label="Link to problem" class="absolute -left-6 opacity-0 group-hover:opacity-100 transition-opacity text-muted-foreground hover:text-foreground no-underline select-none"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" class="lucide lucide-hash size-4" aria-hidden="true"><line x1="4" x2="20" y1="9" y2="9"></line><line x1="4" x2="20" y1="15" y2="15"></line><line x1="10" x2="8" y1="3" y2="21"></line><line x1="16" x2="14" y1="3" y2="21"></line></svg></a><h2 id="problem" class="font-bold tracking-tight text-foreground scroll-mt-24 text-xl sm:text-2xl lg:text-3xl py-5">Problem</h2></div><p class="mb-4">Before ES6, JavaScript codebases relied heavily on <code>var</code> for variable declarations. Due to <code>var</code>'s scoping issues, <code>let</code> and <code>const</code> were introduced—but many codebases still haven't migrated.</p><p class="mb-4">Refactoring manually is tedious and error-prone. Simple find-and-replace won't work either, because there are edge cases where blindly swapping <code>var</code> for <code>const</code> would break your code.</p><p class="mb-4">This is a perfect job for a codemod. We'll build a <code>no-vars</code> transform that automatically converts <code>var</code> declarations to <code>const</code> or <code>let</code> wherever it's safe to do so.</p><p class="mb-4"><strong>Before:</strong></p><div class="relative my-4 overflow-hidden rounded-lg border border-border"><button type="button" tabindex="0" data-slot="tooltip-trigger" aria-label="Copy code" id="base-ui-_r_1_" class="ring-offset-background inline-flex cursor-pointer items-center justify-center gap-2 rounded-md text-sm font-medium whitespace-nowrap transition-colors focus:ring focus:ring-offset-2 focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring focus-visible:ring-offset-2 focus-visible:outline-none disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50 [&>svg]:pointer-events-none [&>svg]:size-4 [&>svg]:shrink-0 hover:bg-accent hover:text-accent-foreground active:bg-accent active:text-accent-foreground focus:ring-accent size-6 p-1 absolute top-3 right-3 z-10"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" class="lucide lucide-copy size-4" aria-hidden="true"><rect width="14" height="14" x="8" y="8" rx="2" ry="2"></rect><path d="M4 16c-1.1 0-2-.9-2-2V4c0-1.1.9-2 2-2h10c1.1 0 2 .9 2 2"></path></svg><span class="sr-only">Copy code</span></button><div><div class="p-3 bg-background text-sm"><div role="presentation" data-slot="scroll-area" class="relative overflow-hidden size-full" style="position: relative; class="payload-richtext"><p class="mb-4">In tree-sitter's JavaScript grammar, <code>for (var x of items)</code> does <strong>not</strong> wrap <code>var x</code> in a <code>variable_declaration</code> node like a normal <code>var</code> statement would. Instead, <code>var</code> and the identifier are bare children of the <code>for_in_statement</code>. We handle this by matching structurally with <code>for (var $X of $Y) $BODY</code> and replacing only the keyword token at <code>child(2)</code> — no regex, no full-node text replacement. This is the kind of detail you'll discover when exploring AST structures in <a target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" class="underline underline-offset-[3px] inline-flex items-center gap-0.5 group pr-0.5 text-foreground" href="https://app.codemod.com/studio">Codemod Studio<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" class="lucide lucide-arrow-up-right size-4 group-hover:translate-x-[2px] group-hover:-translate-y-[2px] transition-transform ease-out" aria-hidden="true"><path d="M7 7h10v10"></path><path d="M7 17 17 7"></path></svg></a>—always verify your assumptions against the actual tree!</p><p class="mb-4"><strong>Note: </strong>When writing codemods, always consider having fallbacks for undesirable cases. Here we chose <code>let</code> as a fallback when <code>const</code> is not applicable, rather than keeping <code>var</code>, since <code>let</code> is arguably the better default.</p></div></div></div><div class="relative group flex items-center"><a href="#testing-the-codemod" aria-label="Link to testing-the-codemod" class="absolute -left-6 opacity-0
